docs.title: False Dealing
docs.text: Sources: CC4 Ch. 46 (pp. 929–944)

docs.title: What False Dealing Is
docs.text:
False dealing refers to the sleight-of-hand techniques used by card cheats to deal from positions other than the top of the deck while appearing to deal normally from the top. The two primary techniques are:

  • The Second Deal: Dealing the second card while retaining the top card
  • The Bottom Deal: Dealing from the bottom of the deck while appearing to deal from the top

These techniques are the province of the card cheat — they are used in gambling to deal good cards to accomplices or the cheat themselves while giving spectators or opponents apparently random cards. In magic, they are used in gambling demonstrations and in specific tricks where dealing from a controlled position is needed.

docs.title: The Cards and The Grip (CC4 Ch. 46)
docs.text:
Giobbi opens this chapter with an observation about the physical demands: false dealing is among the most technically challenging sleights, requiring extremely sensitive finger control and extensive practice. The style of grip used dramatically affects which deals are possible.

The Basic Grip: A variation of the dealing position where the deck is held slightly more firmly, with specific finger contacts that allow the top card to be retained while the second card (or bottom card) is freed.

The True Deal: Before learning false deals, Giobbi establishes what a genuine deal looks like — again, the principle of establishing baseline handling before the false action.

docs.title: The Second Deal
docs.text: The second deal retains the top card (a known card, often a force card or a set card) while dealing the second card from the top. The audience believes the top card is being dealt.

docs.title: The Push-off Second Deal
docs.text:
The left thumb pushes the top card to the right (as in a normal deal). But instead of the right hand taking the top card, the right thumb contacts the second card (now exposed below the pushed-off top card) and deals it. The left thumb pulls the top card back to its position.

The push-off is the more visible version — the top card is visibly pushed right — and requires that this push be identical to the genuine deal.

docs.title: The Strike Second Deal
docs.text:
The top card is not pushed off. Instead, the right hand strikes at the deck from above, and the right thumb contacts the second card from the front rather than taking the top card. The top card remains in place.

The strike version is considered more deceptive because the top card never moves, but it is more difficult to execute cleanly.

docs.title: The Bottom Deal
docs.text:
The bottom card is dealt instead of the top card. The left hand's fingers peel the bottom card outward while the left thumb pushes the top card to the right as a cover (making it look like a top deal); the right hand takes the bottom card.

The bottom deal is used in demonstrations of "bottom dealing" for card cheats — dealing yourself the known card from the bottom.

docs.title: The Sail
docs.text: A variation of the bottom deal where the bottom card is "sailed" out from the deck in a more extended, graceful motion. Used in some theatrical demonstrations.

docs.title: Practical Application in Magic
docs.text:
False dealing is rarely used in casual close-up magic — the techniques are demanding and the practical applications are limited. They are most used in:

  • Gambling demonstrations: The performer demonstrates how card cheats stack and deal, showing the audience exactly how they would be cheated
  • Specific set-up tricks: Where a pre-arranged deck must be dealt in a controlled order

The trick Call Your Hand (CC4) builds a complete poker-demonstration routine around false dealing.

docs.title: The Historical Context: Erdnase
docs.text: The techniques of second and bottom dealing are documented extensively in S.W. Erdnase's The Expert at the Card Table (1902), which remains the primary historical source. Giobbi's treatment builds on Erdnase but incorporates 20th-century refinements. The strike second deal in particular was refined by numerous 20th-century card workers. See S.W. Erdnase.

docs.title: False Shuffles — Overview
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Chs. 2, 7; CC2 Ch. 17; CC3 Ch. 33; CC5 Ch. 55

docs.title: What Makes a Shuffle "False"
docs.text:
A genuine shuffle randomizes the order of the deck. A false shuffle looks identical to a genuine shuffle but secretly preserves some or all of the deck's order. The deception is not about speed — it's about exploiting the spectator's inability to track card-by-card positions through a shuffle.

False shuffles are used to: - Maintain a set-up deck (a stacked order) through an apparent mixing - Keep a controlled card in its known position during handling - Preserve a small stock at the top or bottom while genuinely mixing the rest

docs.title: The Spectrum of Deception
docs.text:
Not all false shuffles are equal. Giobbi organizes them implicitly by what they preserve:

Shuffle What It Preserves Injog overhand shuffle A single card's position Top/bottom stock control Several cards at one end Lift shuffle Top or bottom stock G.W. Hunter shuffle Entire deck Optical shuffle Entire deck (visual deception only) Pull-through shuffle Entire deck (riffle) Zarrow shuffle Entire deck (riffle, tabled) Triumph shuffle Specific arrangement for effect use Perfect faro shuffle Mathematical order preservation

docs.title: Cultural Context
docs.text:
Giobbi repeatedly notes that the type of shuffle appropriate for a performance depends on the cultural context:

  • Overhand shuffle is standard in Europe and casual settings worldwide
  • Riffle shuffle is standard in North American card culture and among poker players
  • Using the "wrong" shuffle for your audience creates mild suspicion — not because of technical failure, but because it doesn't look normal

A European performer performing for American poker players should learn riffle false shuffles. A Japanese performer may use yet different handling norms.

docs.title: The Faro Shuffle
docs.text: Sources: CC3 Ch. 35 (pp. 681–701)

docs.title: What This Is
docs.text:
The Faro Shuffle (also called the Perfect Shuffle or Weave Shuffle) is a riffle shuffle in which the two halves of the deck interleave perfectly — every card from one half sits between two cards from the other half, alternating with mathematical precision. Unlike the Zarrow Shuffle or Pull-Through (which are false shuffles), the Faro genuinely mixes the cards — but in a completely predictable, reversible pattern.

This predictability is what makes the Faro one of the most powerful tools in structured card magic: the performer can know exactly where every card will be after a specific number of Faro shuffles.

docs.title: The In-Shuffle
docs.text: The two halves interleave such that the first card of the right packet goes between the top card and second card of the left packet. The result: the original top card is now in position 2. This "pushes" the deck inward.

docs.title: The Out-Shuffle
docs.text:
The two halves interleave such that the original top card remains on top and the original bottom card remains on the bottom. Everything in between shifts one position toward the center.

The distinction is critical: eight perfect out-shuffles return a 52-card deck to its original order. Eight perfect in-shuffles do the same. Mixing in-shuffles and out-shuffles produces different mathematical outcomes.

docs.title: The Partial Faro (CC3 Ch. 35)
docs.text: A Faro shuffle performed with only part of the deck — one complete half and a partial packet. Used to place a known card at a specific position within a larger portion of the deck.

docs.title: The Faro Slough-off (CC3 Ch. 35)
docs.text: A technique for completing the Faro by pushing the two interlocked halves together in a "slough-off" action. This is not a mechanical action but a handling refinement for squaring the deck cleanly after the interleave.

docs.title: The Incomplete Faro Control (CC3 Ch. 35)
docs.text: Using a partially-completed Faro as a control — the interleave stops at a specific point, allowing the performer to know exactly where a particular card has arrived.

docs.title: The Mathematical Basis (CC3 Ch. 35)
docs.text:
Giobbi includes a detailed treatment of the mathematics. Key facts:

  • Eight out-shuffles return the deck to its original order
  • After n out-shuffles, the card originally at position k moves to position k × 2^n (mod 51)
  • The Faro can be used to stack any card to any position in a known number of shuffles
  • 26 in-shuffles return the deck to its original order (different number due to the position shift)

This mathematics is the foundation of many "mathematical" card tricks where cards appear at impossible positions after a series of shuffles.

docs.title: Performing the Perfect Faro
docs.text:
Executing a perfect Faro requires: 1. The deck must be split into exactly 26 cards in each half — not approximately, but precisely 2. The corners of the two halves must align perfectly before the weave 3. The right-hand packet must push through the left-hand packet with even pressure so every card interleaves with its neighbor

In practice, the Faro is one of the most technically demanding techniques in card magic. Even minor variations in card condition (soft, old, or slightly damaged cards) make a perfect Faro impossible.

Giobbi recommends that new cards, broken in only slightly, are essential. He also notes that the feel of the weave is specific — it should feel like a zipper closing, not a push through resistance.

docs.title: Applications in Tricks
docs.text:
Three complete tricks built on Faro mathematics appear in CC3:

  • The Acrobatic Aces: A multi-phase ace routine using Faro positioning
  • The Two Detectives: A story trick using Faro principles for placement
  • Numerology: A counting effect derived entirely from Faro mathematics

docs.title: Debate: Practicality of the Faro
docs.text:
Giobbi is unusually candid about the limitations: the Faro is not universally practical. It requires new cards in good condition, a quiet environment (the shuffle makes a distinctive sound), and a high level of technical skill. Many professional performers prefer stacking methods or mathematical set-ups that do not require perfect Faro execution.

The counterargument: once mastered, the Faro creates effects of seemingly impossible mathematical precision that no other method can match.

docs.title: What Flourishes Are
docs.text:
Flourishes are display moves — spectacular or elegant handling actions performed for visual effect rather than deception. They demonstrate the performer's mastery of the cards and serve as entertainment in their own right.

Giobbi treats flourishes pragmatically: they are useful tools in a performer's repertoire, but they are not magic. Their role is to establish credibility (the performer can obviously handle cards) and to provide variety — they give the performance moments of visual dynamism that are different from the quiet precision of sleight work.

docs.title: The Philosophical Debate About Flourishes
docs.text:
There is a long-standing debate in card magic about whether flourishes belong in close-up performance:

Against flourishes: They signal that the performer is "skilled with cards," which may make audiences more skeptical of subsequent card handling. If the performer can do this with cards, they can do other things too — which makes spectators watch more carefully. Dai Vernon famously preferred no flourishes in his close-up work.

For flourishes: They provide entertainment between effects, they give the performer something confident to do during patter, and they establish a character — someone who loves cards and works with them constantly. Juan Tamariz's approach embraces flourishes as part of an expressive performance style.

Giobbi's position: Flourishes have their place, but should be used with intention. The performer should know why they are doing a flourish — what it adds to the performance — rather than doing it reflexively.

docs.title: Turning Over the Top Card (I, II, III)
docs.text: Three methods for casually turning the top card of the deck face up: snapping it up with the fingers, flipping it with the thumb, and a more elegant sweep. These establish the performer's ease with the cards.

docs.title: The Charlier Cut
docs.text:
Attributed to: Charlier

A one-handed cut in which the top half of the deck falls through the thumb into the palm, then rises and is dropped onto the bottom half. This is the classic one-handed cut — recognizable, elegant, and surprisingly simple once the finger mechanics are understood.

docs.title: The Swivel Cut
docs.text: A variation of the one-handed cut with a different mechanical approach — the packet swivels on the index finger rather than falling through.

docs.title: The Boomerang Card
docs.text: A single card thrown and "returned" through a spin — a performance gag demonstrating card control.

docs.title: The Spring Riffle Shuffle
docs.text: Cards are sprung from one hand to the other in an arc, creating the "spring" action characteristic of cards with appropriate stiffness and spring. Primarily a visual display.

docs.title: Jack Merlin's Riffle Shuffle
docs.text:
Attributed to: Jack Merlin

A one-handed riffle shuffle where the deck is shuffled against the thumb — a distinctive style. Noted as both a flourish and as a practical (if unusual) false-shuffle method.

docs.title: The Pop-up Card
docs.text: A single card pops upward from the deck through pressure from the fingers below.

docs.title: The Two-handed Fan
docs.text: Spreading the deck into a perfect, symmetrical fan with both hands — one of the most beautiful standard displays in card magic.

docs.title: The Broad One-handed Fan
docs.text: A fan produced with one hand only — the left thumb opens the spread while the fingers curl to spread the cards.

docs.title: The Reverse One-handed Fan
docs.text: A one-handed fan opened in the reverse direction for a different visual effect.

docs.title: The Ribbon Spread Turnover
docs.text: After a ribbon spread across the table, a wave is sent through the spread from one end, flipping all cards face-up in sequence. A dramatic display used as a finale to certain effects.

docs.title: Turning Over the Top Card (IV, V, VI)
docs.text: Three more variations on the top card display — expanding the performer's range.

docs.title: Springing the Cards
docs.text: Cards are sprung from one hand to the other in a continuous stream — a theatrical display of card spring.

docs.title: The Cascade
docs.text: Cards cascade from one hand to the other in a waterfall effect. The cascade is the same action as the Waterfall Riffle Shuffle but performed as a display rather than a shuffle.

docs.title: The Swivelleroo Cut
docs.text: A multi-packet one-handed cut with a swiveling motion.

docs.title: The Flip-flap Cut
docs.text: A compound cut using a flip and a flap of packets.

docs.title: One-handed Riffle Shuffle
docs.text: A complete riffle shuffle performed with one hand — theatrical and impressive.

docs.title: The Side Shuffle
docs.text: A shuffling flourish performed from the side.

docs.title: The Wave Cut, Toss Cut, Twin Falls Shuffle
docs.text: More compound and theatrical cutting and shuffling displays.

docs.title: The Four-card Turnover Display, Star Turnover Display, Frank Shields Display
docs.text: Small packet display flourishes — ways of turning over or displaying a small group of cards elegantly.

docs.title: The Pirouette, Twirl, Ravelli Pop-out
docs.text: More theatrical single-card and packet moves building on the Charlier and Swivel foundations.

  • Fundamentals — ribbon spread and handling
  • Performance Theory — the role of flourishes in presentation

docs.title: What a Force Is
docs.text:
A force is a technique that leads a spectator to "freely" choose a card predetermined by the performer, while the spectator believes their selection was entirely their own choice.

The difference between a force and a control: with a control, the spectator genuinely selects any card and the performer manages its location afterward. With a force, the performer determines which card is selected in the first place.

Forces are among the most powerful tools in card magic because they eliminate the need to "find" a card — the performer already knows what the spectator has before the trick begins.

docs.title: Why Forces Work
docs.text:
Forces exploit several psychological principles:

Free will illusion: Spectators believe they have complete freedom of choice. In most force situations, this belief is genuine — the spectator has not been forced to do something they could not resist. The illusion of free choice is not coercive; it's a matter of design.

Limited tracking: During fast or fluid actions (a riffle, a spread, a dribble), spectators cannot track individual card positions. The performer exploits specific moments when the eye cannot follow what the hand is doing.

Psychological moment: The classic force exploits the observation that in a spreading action, spectators tend to remove a card from the center-right area — and they do this with predictable consistency.

docs.title: Mechanical Forces
docs.text: The force depends on a specific physical maneuver that gives the spectator only one option, even though it appears they have many. The Riffle Force is the clearest example: the card the spectator stops at has been positioned there.

docs.title: Psychological Forces
docs.text: The force works by understanding what spectators tend to do naturally. The Classic Force is the primary example: the deck is spread, and the spectator will most often take from a specific region.

docs.title: Mathematical Forces
docs.text: The force works through counting or mathematical structure. The Ten-to-twenty force (CC1 Ch. 5) uses arithmetic to ensure a specific card is reached regardless of where the spectator begins counting.

docs.title: Volume 1, Chapter 5 — First Forces
docs.text:
The Crisscross Force (a.k.a. the Balducci Force / Cross-cut Force) A spectator cuts the deck and the top section is turned crosswise. After a moment of discussion, the performer points to the card "at the cut" — which is actually the original top card of the deck. This force relies entirely on psychology: the brief delay causes the spectator to forget the original position of the two halves. Giobbi attributes this to Ed Balducci. It is widely known; savvy audiences may have seen it.

The Goldin Force A force using a specific spreading action attributed to Goldin. The performer spreads cards and positions the force card for the spectator to take.

The Ten-to-twenty Force A mathematical force: the spectator thinks of a number between 10 and 20, counts down that many cards, adds the digits of their number, and counts down that many more — arriving at the force card.

docs.title: Volume 1, Chapter 15 — The Major Forces
docs.text:
The Classic Force — see The Classic Force

The Riffle Force — see The Riffle Force

docs.title: Volume 4, Chapter 39 — Advanced Forces
docs.text: See Other Forces for: - The Under-the-Table Force - The Ribbon-spread Force - The Toss Force - The Peek Force - The Cut Force - The Credit-card Force - The Packet Force - The Mishmash Force - The Bottom Bluff Force - The Dribble Force

docs.title: Outs (CC1 Ch. 15)
docs.text:
A crucial concept: no force succeeds 100% of the time. An out is an alternative procedure the performer uses when a force fails — a way to pivot so the trick still works even though the spectator took the wrong card.

Giobbi discusses outs explicitly in CC1 Ch. 15: - Direct outs: The performer immediately adapts the trick to whatever card was taken - Indirect outs: The performer uses a brief moment of misdirection to switch the taken card for the force card

Performers who know their outs approach the Classic Force with confidence rather than anxiety — and that confidence itself helps the force succeed.

docs.title: Force vs. Control: The Philosophy
docs.text:
Giobbi reflects throughout the series that forces are generally superior to controls when the effect allows. A force means: - No need to locate the card - No risk of the card being lost - Cleaner structure: the performer chooses what happens before the trick begins

The downside: forces require setup (positioning the card in advance) and the force method must match the performance context. A force visible to one spectator cannot be used for a group.

docs.title: Fundamental Techniques
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Ch. 1 (pp. 13–36); CC1 Introduction

docs.title: What This Covers
docs.text:
This article covers the non-sleight handling skills that every card worker must own before any sleight makes sense: squaring the deck, spreading cards, dribbling, dealing, and the ribbon spread. These techniques have no deception in themselves — their purpose is to make the performer's handling look fluid, natural, and professional at all times.

Giobbi opens Card College with these basics not as a formality but as a philosophical statement: the baseline of handling must look identical to the sleight. If your double lift looks different from a normal turnover, the audience will sense it even if they cannot name it. The fundamentals set the standard against which all sleights are measured.

docs.title: Squaring the Deck
docs.text:
The deck must be kept perfectly squared at all times except when deliberately spread or fanned. Giobbi teaches the All-around Square-up: the right hand approaches from above and the fingers and thumb simultaneously tamp all four sides of the deck into alignment. This full-perimeter squaring looks thorough and natural, and it doubles as a cover for various break acquisitions and card placements.

Sloppy squaring is a chronic fault in amateur card workers. An unsquared deck invites spectators to wonder what is between the cards.

docs.title: Spreading Cards Between the Hands
docs.text:
Cards are spread from the left hand into the right by pushing them with the left thumb while the right fingers receive them and space them evenly. This hand-to-hand spread is used constantly for displaying cards and for allowing a spectator to select one.

Outjogging cards during a spread: As cards are spread, specific cards can be pushed inward or outward by the left thumb without breaking the rhythm of the spread. This is the basis of the spread cull and many other techniques.

docs.title: Dribbling Cards
docs.text:
Cards are released from the deck in a continuous stream from right hand to left (or onto the table). The right thumb releases cards from the inner end while the fingers provide resistance. Dribbling serves as:

  • A fair-seeming way to let a spectator "call stop"
  • Cover for the dribble break
  • A component of the dribble force and dribble pass
  • A visual display of handling ease

docs.title: Dealing Cards
docs.text:
Two dealing methods are established early:

  • Draw method (face down): The left thumb pushes the top card to the right, and the right hand takes it by its right edge and deals it face down — the standard poker deal
  • Stud method (face up): The right hand takes the card face down, then flips it face up as it is placed on the table

The distinction matters because different tricks use different dealing styles, and performing the wrong one mid-trick creates visual inconsistency.

docs.title: The Ribbon Spread
docs.text:
Cards are spread in a smooth, even line across a close-up pad or table. A clean ribbon spread requires:

  1. Placing the deck at the left end of the pad at a 45° angle to the line of travel
  2. Dragging rightward while applying even downward pressure
  3. Keeping spacing uniform throughout

The ribbon spread is used to display a full deck, to allow free selection, to table a card face down for later revelation, and as part of the ribbon spread turnover (where all cards are flipped face up in a wave — a flourish taught in CC1 Ch. 12).

docs.title: The Close-up Pad
docs.text:
Giobbi advocates strongly for using a close-up pad (a felt or velvet mat). The pad:

  • Allows the ribbon spread to work smoothly
  • Gives the hands a consistent work surface
  • Prevents cards from sliding
  • Looks professional

This is one of the few instances where Giobbi recommends a specific prop beyond the cards themselves.

docs.title: A Brief History of Playing Cards (CC1 pp. 5–7)
docs.text:
Giobbi opens with historical context: playing cards likely originated in China in the 9th century CE, migrated through Persia and Egypt, and arrived in Europe by the late 14th century. The four-suit system (hearts, diamonds, clubs, spades) is a French innovation from around 1480. Modern poker-size cards (2.5" × 3.5") became the standard for sleight of hand in the 20th century.

Understanding the history gives the card worker a richer connection to the tradition they are participating in — and provides material for presentations.

docs.text:

  • Flourishes — ribbon spread turnover, fans, springs

docs.title: The Glide
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Ch. 8 (pp. 119–126)

docs.title: What the Glide Is
docs.text:
The Glide is a simple and elegant sleight performed with the deck held face down in the left hand. When the performer appears to deal the bottom card, the left middle finger secretly pulls the bottom card back a fraction of an inch — and the card that is actually dealt is the second card from the bottom, not the bottom card.

The effect: the performer appears to deal from a known position (the face of the deck), but is actually dealing a different card from what was shown.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The deck is held face down in the left hand with the cards angled toward the floor — the standard glide position:

  • The left thumb is at the upper left edge
  • The left fingers curl around the lower edge, with the middle finger resting lightly on the back of the face card
  • The deck is tipped at a slight angle so the face card's face can be shown to the audience

To execute the glide:

  1. The face card is shown to the audience ("This is the three of clubs")
  2. The left middle finger curls inward, drawing the face card back about half an inch while the hand returns to the horizontal position
  3. The right thumb reaches under the deck and takes the now-exposed second card from the bottom, dealing it face down

To the audience, the bottom card (the named card) was dealt. In reality, it remains in the hand and a different card was dealt.

To end the glide and deal the actual face card, the middle finger pushes the bottom card back into alignment and it is dealt normally.

docs.title: Applications
docs.text:
Deals and misdirections: The glide allows the performer to appear to deal a specific card while actually dealing a neutral card, or vice versa.

The Under-the-spread Force: Related technique from CC1 Ch. 13 — the glide's principle is applied during a spread to force a specific card.

The Glide Change: The glide principle is also used in the card-switch context (CC4 Ch. 40) — see Card Switches.

docs.title: Classic Tricks Using the Glide (CC1 Ch. 8)
docs.text:
• The Acrobat Family: A three-of-a-kind plot where cards appear and disappear using the glide as the mechanism
  • The Coue Test: A presentation built around suggestion and "autosuggestion" theory, using the glide as the hidden engine

These tricks appear in CC1 as illustrations of the glide's utility in performance.

docs.title: Notes on Naturalness
docs.text: The glide is performed with the deck held at an unusual angle (tilted toward the performer). Giobbi addresses this: the angled position must be introduced naturally and maintained consistently so it does not look like a suspicious "trick position." The best way is to show the face of the deck as part of the presentation — the angle of showing the face is the same angle needed for the glide.

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:

  • Controls — Overview
  • Hand Positions and Basic Grips

docs.title: The Glimpse
docs.text: Sources: CC2 Ch. 23 (pp. 353–363)

docs.title: What the Glimpse Is
docs.text:
A glimpse is a secret look at the face of a card — learning its identity without the audience knowing. Once the performer knows a card's identity, that card becomes a key card or a force reference point.

Unlike the key card (where the performer places a known card next to the selection), the glimpse is done after the selection: the performer sees the selection (or a specific card) and uses that knowledge to perform the location or revelation.

docs.title: Top-card Riffle Glimpse
docs.text:
The most useful and natural-looking glimpse method. As the performer rifles the corner of the deck, the top card is held slightly apart — and the performer's downward angle reveals the face of the top card during the riffle action. From the front, the performer appears to be simply riffling the cards.

Used constantly in practice: any time the deck is riffled (for a force, for a spread, for any reason), the top card can be glimpsed without any additional handling.

docs.title: Top-card Double Lift Glimpse
docs.text:
During the action of a double lift — as the two cards are turned over — the second card's face is briefly visible to the performer at the inner end. This glimpse reveals what card is second from the top.

This makes the double lift doubly useful: it both shows a false card to the audience and simultaneously reveals the hidden card to the performer.

docs.title: Top-card Riffle Shuffle Glimpse
docs.text: During a genuine riffle shuffle, the top card can be seen at the end of the shuffle as packets are squared. A specific handling angle allows this without any additional preparation.

docs.title: Bottom Card — All-around Square-up Glimpse
docs.text:
During the all-around square-up action (squaring the deck from all four sides), the deck is momentarily tilted enough that the bottom card's face is seen by the performer.

This is one of the most natural and brief glimpse moments: the squaring action is completely expected and unremarkable; the glimpse is a byproduct of a normal handling.

docs.title: Situational Bottom Card Glimpses
docs.text: Giobbi describes several situation-dependent glimpses: moments during ordinary handling — placing the deck on the table, picking it up, handing it to a spectator — where the bottom card's face naturally comes into the performer's view. These require no technique; they require awareness of when to look.

docs.title: Center-card Glimpse
docs.text: Seeing a card in the middle of the deck. This is more complex than a top or bottom glimpse. Methods include: - Spreading the deck and using the angle of the spread - Using a peek (having the spectator look at a card in the middle while the performer glimpses it from the back)

docs.title: The Glimpse as a Habitual Skill
docs.text:
Giobbi makes a strong philosophical point: the glimpse is not just a technique — it is a perceptual habit. Experienced card workers are always gathering information passively during normal handling. They glimpse the top card when riffling, the bottom card when squaring, and cards at specific positions during dealing — not because they are executing a deliberate technique, but because they have trained themselves to notice.

This constant awareness is one of the key differences between novice and expert card workers. The expert always knows more about the current state of the deck than they appear to.

docs.text:

  • The Riffle Force — riffle glimpse creates the force
  • Double Lift — double lift glimpse

docs.title: Hand Positions and Basic Grips
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Ch. 1 (pp. 13–36)

docs.title: What This Is
docs.text: Before any sleight can be learned, a card worker needs a consistent, natural way to hold the deck. Giobbi opens Card College with three fundamental grip positions because every technique in the five-volume course builds on them. These are not sleights — they are the neutral, resting positions from which sleights begin and to which the hands return.

docs.title: Dealing Position (Left Hand)
docs.text:
The most important grip in card magic. The deck lies face down across the left hand, held by four main pressure points:

  • Left thumb rests on top, pointing toward the outer right corner
  • Left fingers curl under the deck; the middle, ring, and little fingers press against the right long edge
  • Left index finger wraps around the outer short end of the deck and presses lightly upward on the face
  • The deck rests in the fork of the thumb, in the flesh between thumb and index finger

The deck should be comfortable and under complete control. The left thumb does most of the work — it can push cards right (for a spread), and it can press down to maintain even pressure across the top. From dealing position, nearly every left-hand sleight originates.

docs.title: End Grip (Right Hand)
docs.text:
The right hand holds the deck at its short ends (the "ends" in card magic terminology, as opposed to the long sides which are called "sides" or "edges"):

  • Thumb contacts the inner short end (the end closest to the performer)
  • Middle, ring, and little fingers contact the outer short end
  • Index finger curls over and rests lightly on top of the deck

This grip is used whenever the deck passes to the right hand for a shuffle, cut, or display. It gives maximum control at the ends for riffling and for producing breaks.

docs.title: Elevated Dealing Position
docs.text: A variation of dealing position where the deck is held higher in the hand — toward the fingertips rather than deep in the palm. This becomes relevant for certain palming techniques where a deeper palm grip would expose the back of the hand uncomfortably.

docs.title: Why These Positions Matter
docs.text:
Giobbi is insistent on precision here because consistency creates invisibility. A spectator who sees the deck held the same way at all times has no visual trigger to notice when something changes. The moment a performer tightens their grip awkwardly or shifts the deck into an unusual position, attention focuses on the hands.

The principle: the rest position should look identical to the action position.

docs.title: The Swing Cut
docs.text: Also taught in Chapter 1, the swing cut is the natural, one-handed cut most people do automatically. The top half of the deck pivots outward on the left thumb, swings to the left, and is placed below the bottom half. It appears casual because it is casual — and that quality makes it the preferred method for cutting under normal circumstances.

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:

  • Fundamentals — squaring, spreading, and the ribbon spread
  • Palming — how palming modifies the grip without looking different

docs.title: The Hindu Shuffle
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Ch. 11 (pp. 153–165)

docs.title: What the Hindu Shuffle Is
docs.text:
The Hindu Shuffle is a style of shuffling cards originating in South Asia, widely used throughout Asia and recognized in many parts of the world. The right hand holds the deck by the sides (not the ends) and lifts it; the left thumb and fingers grasp small packets from the top and let them fall into the left palm repeatedly, until the deck is exhausted.

Visually, the Hindu Shuffle looks completely different from the overhand or riffle shuffle — cards fall in small clumps downward rather than being peeled off the top. For audiences familiar with it, it reads as a natural shuffle.

docs.title: Technique
docs.text:
The deck starts in the right hand, held by the sides between thumb and fingers. The left hand approaches from below. The left thumb and second finger contact the top packet; the right hand drops away slightly, releasing that top packet into the left hand. The left hand accepts the packet. The right hand moves to take the next packet. This continues until all packets have fallen into the left hand.

The shuffle is completely genuine in its basic form — it produces real mixing.

docs.title: Placing a Key Card with a Hindu Shuffle
docs.text: By controlling when the left hand accepts or releases its packet, the performer can place a key card at any desired position. This is the first control taught with the Hindu Shuffle.

docs.title: Glimpsing the Bottom Card with a Hindu Shuffle
docs.text: As the last packet falls into the left hand during a genuine shuffle, the bottom card of that final packet can be briefly seen by the performer (glimpsed) before the deck is squared. This provides a key card without any overt preparation.

docs.title: The Hindu Shuffle Force
docs.text:
One of the most versatile forces in card magic:

  1. The force card is on the bottom of the deck
  2. The performer begins a genuine Hindu Shuffle
  3. The spectator is asked to "call stop" at any point
  4. When the spectator says stop, the performer extends the remaining packet in the right hand — the bottom card (the force card) is now the face of this packet
  5. "Is this your card?" — The spectator appears to have stopped the shuffle freely

The Hindu Shuffle Force has a high success rate and is deceptive because the stopping point is genuinely free and the force card is genuinely at whatever position the spectator stopped. The only condition: the force card must be on the bottom and the performer must continue shuffling until after the spectator says stop.

docs.title: Controlling a Single Card
docs.text: A card is returned to the deck as packets are being dropped. The performer lifts the right hand's remaining packet and the returned card is now between packets in a controlled position. A final shuffle run maneuvers it to the top.

docs.title: Controlling Multiple Cards
docs.text: The same concept extended — multiple cards returned to the deck are kept as a group and shuttled to the top.

docs.title: Cultural Context
docs.text: Giobbi notes that the Hindu Shuffle's cultural origin affects its reception in performance. For non-Western audiences, it may look completely natural. For Western audiences, it may look unusual or foreign — which can either add interest ("how do you do that?") or arouse suspicion ("why is he doing it that way?"). The performer should choose their shuffle style based on their audience.

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:

  • False Shuffles — Overview
  • Forces — Overview — Hindu Shuffle Force is one of the most useful
  • The Glimpse

docs.title: The Invisible Pass
docs.text: Sources: CC4 Ch. 49 (pp. 973–975)

docs.title: What This Is
docs.text:
The Invisible Pass is not a different mechanical pass — it uses the same transposition action as the Classic Pass. What makes it "invisible" is a specific cover strategy: the pass is timed to occur simultaneously with a natural, larger action that completely conceals any visible evidence of the exchange.

The name comes from the performance ideal: if executed correctly, there is nothing to see. Not speed, not blur — literally nothing. The deck simply appears to be handled normally, and yet a card has been moved.

docs.title: The Core Principle
docs.text:
Giobbi articulates the principle clearly: any action that occupies the spectators' attention for one fraction of a second provides enough cover for the pass. The pass does not need to be fast. It needs to be timed to an action that draws attention elsewhere.

Possible cover actions taught in CC4 Ch. 49:

  • Turning to address a different spectator — the body turn provides complete cover; the pass occurs during the pivot
  • Picking up the deck from the table — the moment of lifting provides cover
  • Placing the deck down — similarly provides cover
  • Spreading the deck — the spreading action draws the eye to the spread; pass occurs during the spread initiation
  • Squaring — the most classic cover; the squaring action is so expected that no one looks at it critically

docs.title: Why "Invisible" Is the Right Goal
docs.text:
A common mistake in learning the pass is to try to make it fast. Speed produces a visible blur — something happened, even if the audience cannot identify what. An invisible pass is not fast: it is timed, covered, and smooth. A pass executed at natural speed during a covered action is literally undetectable because there is nothing to detect.

Giobbi quotes a principle often associated with Vernon's thinking: "Naturalness is the master cover." The invisible pass is the pass done at a natural pace, in a natural context, with the natural action of squaring or handling the deck.

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:
• The Pass — Overview
  • Timing
  • Misdirection and Directing Attention

docs.title: The Key Card
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Ch. 10 (pp. 135–151)

docs.title: What the Key Card Is
docs.text:
A key card is a card whose identity the performer knows, deliberately placed next to a spectator's selection so that the selection can be located later. When the deck is spread or searched, finding the key card identifies the position of the selected card.

The key card concept requires no dexterity whatsoever — it is a principle, not a sleight. A spectator replaces their card in the deck; the performer notes the card above or below the replaced card (the key card); later, the selection can always be found. This makes it ideal for beginners and for situations where sleight of hand is inappropriate.

docs.title: The Key Card in a Hand-to-hand Spread
docs.text: The spectator's selection is replaced in the middle of a hand-to-hand spread. As the spread is closed, the performer glimpses the bottom card of the upper packet — this card will end up directly above the selected card.

docs.title: Key Card in a Ribbon Spread
docs.text: The deck is spread face down across the table. The spectator replaces their card anywhere in the spread. As the performer scoops up the cards, they glimpse the bottom-facing card of the packet just below where the selection was placed.

docs.title: Glimpse and Placement
docs.text: A more deliberate method: the performer secretly glimpses the bottom card of the deck before the selection is made. When the spectator replaces their card, the performer controls it to the bottom — now the key card (the original bottom card) is directly above it. Or the key card is placed on top of the selected card before the deck is cut.

docs.title: Location, Identification and Control with a Key Card
Control in a Hand-to-hand Spread
docs.text: Once the selection is known (by its relationship to the key card), it can be controlled to the top or bottom.

docs.title: Tabled Ribbon Spread Control
docs.text: After the key card reveals the selection's position in a tabled spread, the spread is scooped up in a way that naturally controls the selection.

docs.title: Shuffling with a Key Card
docs.text: Key card placement through shuffling: By controlling the key card's position through overhand shuffle techniques, the key card can be kept adjacent to the selection throughout shuffling. The spectator may even be allowed to shuffle — see below.

docs.title: Letting the Spectator Shuffle with a Key Card
docs.text:
One of the key card's most powerful applications: the spectator is allowed to overhand shuffle the deck themselves. The key card will drift toward the selection rather than away from it — because in most shuffles, adjacent cards remain roughly adjacent. After the shuffle, the performer can still locate the selection.

Giobbi emphasizes that this is not guaranteed, but it works often enough to be worth presenting as a miracle: the deck has been genuinely shuffled by the spectator, yet the performer finds the card.

docs.title: Further Thoughts on the Key Card
docs.text:
Giobbi discusses the philosophical position of the key card technique:

  • It is the simplest location method — no dexterity
  • It is the most limitation-free — works in any situation, including when spectators handle the cards
  • Its weakness: the deck cannot be casually spread face-up (this would reveal the selected card among others the performer is "supposedly" not tracking)
  • Advanced performers combine the key card with slights to get the selected card to a position where a dramatic revelation is possible

docs.title: Tricks Using the Key Card (CC1 Ch. 10)
docs.text:
• A Phenomenal Memory: The performer memorizes all 52 cards' positions; actually uses a key card and a clever structure
  • Your Number — Your Card: Spectator names a number; their card is at that position; key card makes this possible without memorization
  • Thot Echo: A mind-reading effect using a key card

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:
• Controls — Overview
  • The Glimpse — often used to establish the key card
  • Fundamentals — ribbon spread and hand-to-hand spread

docs.title: Lapping
docs.text: Sources: CC4 Ch. 48 (pp. 959–967)

docs.title: What Lapping Is
docs.text:
Lapping is the use of the lap — the space between the performer's thighs when seated at a table — as a hidden holding area. A card (or small packet) is secretly dropped into the lap, where it can be held out of play until needed, then picked up later.

The lap is one of the most practical secret locations in close-up magic because it is completely out of sight when the performer is seated, it requires no technical sleight to use, and a card placed there is genuinely hidden (not palmed in a hand, where it could potentially be seen).

docs.title: General Observations on Lapping (CC4 Ch. 48)
docs.text:
Giobbi opens with the context: lapping is only possible when the performer is seated at a table. It is irrelevant to standing performers. For close-up workers who primarily sit (as at a card table or intimate setting), it is one of the most powerful tools available.

He also notes the psychological advantage: because the lap is completely out of view, there is no angle problem. A palmed card in the right hand must be positioned correctly for every spectator; a lapped card is simply invisible to everyone.

docs.title: The Bottom-card Lap
docs.text:
A card is lapped from the bottom of the deck. The deck is casually placed on the table — or positioned near the table's edge — and the bottom card is allowed to slide into the lap as the deck lands. The bottom card is never seen by spectators.

This is the simplest and most direct lapping method. It requires no additional handling and can be executed casually during conversation.

docs.title: The All-around Square-up Lap
docs.text: During the all-around square-up action (squaring the deck from all four sides), the deck is momentarily brought near the table's edge. One card is released into the lap during the squaring. The all-around action provides complete cover — spectators see normal squaring, not a lap drop.

docs.title: Jean-Claude Pages's Lapping Method
docs.text:
Attributed to: Jean-Claude Pages

A specific lapping method by French close-up worker Jean-Claude Pages. His technique uses a slight body adjustment as cover — a natural forward lean that brings the deck closer to the lap without looking deliberate.

docs.title: The Lap Control
docs.text:
A complete sequence: a card is lapped, the deck is handled normally for a phase or two, and then the lapped card is secretly retrieved from the lap and added back to the deck. The card appears to have "traveled" from the deck, done something impossible, and returned.

Giobbi discusses the psychological importance of timing: the card must be retrieved from the lap at a moment when the hands naturally pass through the lap area — reaching into a pocket, adjusting a jacket, or any motion that brings the hand near the lap.

docs.title: The Trousers-fold Holdout
docs.text: A more elaborate version where a card is held in a fold of the trouser fabric — a small crease that holds the card against the inner thigh. This is a temporary holdout rather than a true lap (the card is not free in the lap but secured against the fabric) and is used when the performer must stand briefly while still needing to hold out a card.

docs.title: The Knee Holdout
docs.text: A card is pressed between the knees while seated. More secure than lapping but less clean to execute and retrieve. Used in specific situations where a lap drop would be difficult.

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:
• Palming — the alternative to lapping for holding cards
  • Stacking — lapping can be part of a deck switch setup
  • Card Switches — Deck Switch

docs.title: Misdirection and Directing Attention
docs.text: Sources: CC2 Ch. 27 (pp. 434–444)

docs.title: What Misdirection Is
docs.text:
Misdirection is the most misunderstood concept in magic. It is often described — incorrectly — as "making the audience look away." Giobbi rejects this definition and offers a more precise one: misdirection is the management of attention — directing where spectators focus, not simply distracting them from the sleight.

The distinction matters profoundly. A performer who thinks of misdirection as "look over there" will create obvious, mechanical diversions. A performer who thinks of it as "design the experience so attention is naturally where it should be" will create seamless, natural-feeling magic.

docs.title: The Physical Direction of Attention
docs.text:
Physical attention follows movement, contrast, and novelty:

  • Movement: The eye tracks motion automatically. A moving hand draws attention away from a stationary one. A moved object keeps spectators' eyes on it
  • Contrast: A bright object, a loud sound, or any sensory contrast draws focus
  • Novelty: Something unexpected or new automatically captures attention
  • The performer's gaze: Spectators follow the performer's eyes. If the performer looks at a spectator, that spectator feels addressed. If the performer looks at an object, spectators look at that object

Pointing as direction: Pointing physically with a finger is the most direct form of attention direction. But pointing can also be implicit — an arrangement of objects on the table can imply direction (three aces arranged in a triangle pointing at a face-down card direct attention at that card without any gesture).

docs.title: Mental Direction of Attention
docs.text:
More subtle and more powerful. Attention is managed not just through physical tools but through psychological context:

  • Questions: Asking the spectator a question occupies their cognitive attention. They cannot simultaneously think about the question and analyze what the hands are doing
  • Numbers: Asking a spectator to remember a number or count something occupies working memory
  • Stories: A narrative being told engages imaginative attention; spectators are partly in the story, not fully in the room
  • Meaningful tasks: Asking the spectator to do something with purpose ("sign your name here," "hold this tightly") gives them a task that occupies attention

docs.title: Reading Your Spectators
docs.text:
An advanced skill: understanding how different spectators are watching. Some spectators are analytical — they watch the hands closely and try to work out the method. Others are experiential — they let themselves be taken along for the ride.

Giobbi describes several spectator types and how to manage each. The analytical spectator requires more misdirection work; the experiential spectator requires less technique but more presentational engagement. Knowing which type you are working with allows the performer to adjust in real time.

docs.title: Strong and Weak Moments
docs.text:
Attributed to: Slydini (Tony Slydini)

The concept of strong and weak moments in a performance sequence. A strong moment is one when audience attention is fully engaged — during a reveal, during the climax, during any high-interest moment. A weak moment is one during a transition, during casual handling, during conversation.

Sleights should be executed during weak moments — when attention is naturally lower. A sleight executed during a strong moment (when everyone is watching carefully) is far more likely to be noticed.

Timing a sleight to a weak moment is the technical application of misdirection. The sequence: create a strong moment (engage attention somewhere else), execute the sleight during the momentary weak moment that follows, then create a new strong moment to move forward.

docs.title: The Concept of "Strong" vs. "Weak" Moments
docs.text:
Slydini's insight, as interpreted by Giobbi: the most important skill in performance is not the sleight itself but knowing when to execute it. A technically imperfect sleight executed at the perfect moment can succeed completely; a perfect sleight executed at the wrong moment can fail.

This is why practice of sleights in isolation (at a mirror, alone) is insufficient. The performer must also practice timing — executing the sleight at the right moment in the flow of a performance, under actual audience conditions.

docs.title: Summary: The Four Tools of Misdirection
docs.text:
1. The performer's gaze — look where you want spectators to look
  2. Movement — move what should be watched; keep still what should be hidden
  3. Words and questions — engage cognitive attention
  4. Strong and weak moments — time sleights to weak moments

docs.title: The Multiple Shift
docs.text: Sources: CC3 Ch. 34 (pp. 661–679)

docs.title: What the Multiple Shift Is
docs.text:
The Multiple Shift (also called the Multiple Pass or the Card Transfer) moves several cards from different positions in the deck to a single destination — usually the top — in one action. It solves the problem of bringing multiple selected cards together when they have been scattered through the deck.

In a trick where three or four spectators each insert their selection into different parts of the deck, the Multiple Shift brings all of them together at the top instantly.

docs.title: The Insertion of the Cards
docs.text:
Before the shift can be executed, the cards must be inserted in a specific way that allows the shift to work. Giobbi teaches how to have spectators insert cards at different depths while the performer maintains a single little-finger break below all of them.

The standard setup: each card is inserted into the spread and a break is maintained below all insertions. The performer then closes the spread and executes the shift.

docs.title: Swing Cut Multiple Shift
docs.text: The multiple cards are brought to the top during a swing cut action. The cut provides cover; at its conclusion, all selected cards are on top.

docs.title: Bluff Multiple Shift
docs.text: The multiple shift is performed as a "bluff" — appearing to be two or three fair cuts, but actually functioning as a shift.

docs.title: The Vernon Multiple Shift
docs.text:
Attributed to: Dai Vernon

Vernon's version of the multiple shift using a specific hand position and weight distribution that makes the move feel (to the performer) more like a natural squaring than a sleight. This is considered the cleanest handling.

docs.title: Cardini's Overhand Multiple Shift
docs.text:
Attributed to: Cardini (Richard Valentine Pitchford)

A version executed during an overhand shuffle. As cards are shuffled, the inserted selections are brought together at the top. The overhand shuffle provides extended cover and looks more casual than the swing cut version.

docs.title: Carney's Natural Multiple Shift
docs.text:
Attributed to: John Carney

A handling developed by American card worker John Carney that emphasizes extreme naturalness — the shift looks like a genuinely casual squaring of the deck.

docs.title: The Simple Shift
docs.text: A streamlined version for fewer cards (two or three) in less complex situations.

docs.title: The Tabled Multiple Shift
docs.text: A version performed on the table — the deck is placed down and the shift occurs during the tabling and squaring action.

docs.title: Attribution Notes
docs.text:
The multiple shift has a long history in card magic, with versions attributed to S.W. Erdnase, Dai Vernon, and others. The specific named versions above are Giobbi's catalog of the most practically useful modern versions. Giobbi attributes each clearly:

  • Vernon Multiple Shift: Dai Vernon
  • Cardini's version: Cardini — the British-born comedy magician famous for his manipulative act
  • Carney's version: John Carney, a modern American performer known for naturalistic technique

docs.text:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Beyond the Classic Force and Riffle Force, Giobbi catalogues a large repertoire of specific force methods throughout the series. This article surveys the less central but practically important forces.

docs.title: The Crisscross Force (Balducci Force / Cross-cut Force)
docs.text:
Attributed to: Ed Balducci

The spectator cuts the deck freely and places the top packet crosswise on the bottom packet. After a brief conversational pause, the performer lifts the top (crosswise) packet and points to the top card of the lower packet as "the card at the cut." This card is, of course, the original top card of the deck before the cut.

The force works entirely through time displacement: the brief pause and conversation cause the spectator to forget the original positions of the two packets. They believe the "card at the cut" was randomly determined by their own cut.

Caution: This force is widely known in the magic world and in the general public. Giobbi advises using it judiciously and never repeating it for the same audience.

docs.title: The Goldin Force
docs.text:
Attributed to: Goldin

A spread-based force that uses a specific mechanical advantage to position the force card. Less detail is given by Giobbi; he presents it as one of several spread-type forces.

docs.title: The Ten-to-twenty Force
docs.text:
A mathematical force that requires no physical dexterity: 1. Ask the spectator to think of a number between 10 and 20 2. Deal that many cards face down 3. Add the two digits of their number (e.g., 17 → 1+7 = 8) 4. Count down that many from the dealt pile 5. The card at that position is the force card

This works because any number from 10 to 20 produces a digit sum between 1 and 11, and the resulting final position always lands on the same card (the 10th card from the original deck top, if set up correctly).

A completely automatic force requiring no dexterity. Its weakness: the spectator watches you deal cards, which can feel procedural and mechanical in a way that undermines the atmosphere of a magic performance.

docs.title: The Under-the-Table Force
docs.text: A force performed with the deck beneath the table, out of sight. The spectator names a number; the performer counts down to that card under the table. The force relies on a specific setup and the performer's ability to count cards by feel.

docs.title: The Ribbon-spread Force
docs.text: The deck is ribbon-spread face down across the table. The spectator touches or slides out a card. Through a specific set-up positioning, the card they most naturally pick up is the force card.

docs.title: The Toss Force
docs.text: The deck is tossed or fanned, and the spectator takes a card in the air or from the fan. The force relies on controlling which card is accessible at the moment the spectator reaches.

docs.title: The Peek Force
docs.text: The spectator "peeks" at a card by pushing the corner open during a riffle. The performer forces the peek position — the card at the peek point is the force card.

docs.title: The Cut Force
docs.text: The spectator cuts the deck and the cut is used to force the position. Related to the Crisscross Force but using a different follow-through.

docs.title: The Credit-card Force
docs.text: The spectator uses a credit card or business card to stop the performer's riffle. The credit card's edge is used to establish the stop point, which the performer controls.

docs.title: The Curious Count
docs.text: A mathematical force based on a counting sequence with a specific setup.

docs.title: The Packet Force
docs.text: A force performed with a small packet of cards rather than the full deck.

docs.title: The Mishmash Force
docs.text: A compound force combining elements of spreading, dealing, and audience interaction.

docs.title: Forcing Under a Handkerchief
docs.text: The force is performed with the deck covered by a handkerchief. A theatrical and unusual approach that adds a layer of impossibility.

docs.title: The Bottom Bluff Force
docs.text: The force card is on the bottom of the deck. A specific handling bluffs the spectator into believing they are selecting from the middle.

docs.title: The Dribble Force
docs.text: Cards are dribbled from one hand to the other and the spectator "stops" the dribble. The force card is positioned to be at the top of the dribbled packet at the moment the spectator most naturally stops.

docs.title: Organizing Principles
docs.text:
Giobbi doesn't rank these forces hierarchically; instead he notes that each is optimal in specific conditions:

  • Large audience or group: Ribbon-spread and tabled forces work better; hand-to-hand forces have angle problems
  • Skeptical audience: Mathematical forces (Ten-to-twenty, Curious Count) remove any apparent skill, making them harder to attribute to technique
  • Theater setting: Under-the-table and handkerchief forces add theatrical atmosphere
  • Casual intimate setting: The Classic Force and Riffle Force remain the gold standard

  • The Riffle Force

docs.title: Overhand False Shuffles
docs.text: Sources: CC1 Ch. 2 (pp. 37–48); CC2 Ch. 17 (pp. 251–265); CC5 Ch. 55

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The overhand shuffle's natural visual complexity makes it ideal for false shuffles — the audience sees cards being mixed, and the human mind does not track individual card positions through the action. Every overhand false shuffle exploits this limitation.

For basics of the genuine overhand shuffle and controls, see Overhand Shuffle Controls.

docs.title: Top and Bottom Stock Controls (CC1 Ch. 2)
docs.text:
The simplest "false shuffles" are stock controls that genuinely mix the middle of the deck while preserving a known packet at the top or bottom.

Controlling the top stock: Run the top cards one at a time (slowly, singly) at the start of the shuffle. This deposits them on the bottom of the left hand's growing packet. Then shuffle off the middle freely. Then run the same number of cards at the end — they return to the top. The net result: top stock preserved, middle genuinely shuffled.

Controlling the bottom stock: The bottom cards are injogged as a block, then the injog is used to cut them back to the bottom at the end of the shuffle. The middle is genuinely mixed.

These are not fully false shuffles — they do mix the middle — but they allow a performer to maintain a setup while appearing to shuffle.

docs.title: The Lift Shuffle (CC2 Ch. 17)
docs.text:
A variation of the overhand where the right hand lifts packets from the bottom of the left hand's accumulation rather than having the left thumb peel from the top. The visual appearance is nearly identical to a standard overhand shuffle, but the mechanics allow different stock preservation moves.

Top stock control with the lift shuffle: The initial packet taken by the right hand is drawn from the bottom of the deck. As the shuffle proceeds, the original top stock ends up on top at the conclusion.

Key card placement: As cards are lifted, the right hand can position a key card at a specific depth by monitoring how many cards have been lifted.

docs.title: The G.W. Hunter Shuffle (CC2 Ch. 17)
docs.text:
Attributed to: G.W. Hunter

This shuffle controls the entire deck to its original order through two overhand passes that cancel each other out. Each pass individually appears to shuffle, but the combined effect returns all cards to their starting positions.

The Hunter shuffle works because of the mathematical properties of the overhand shuffle: shuffling a packet twice in specific ways can restore its original order. Giobbi teaches the exact two-pass sequence.

This is a complete false shuffle — the deck ends exactly as it began — and it looks like a fairly thorough overhand mixing. Its weakness is that experienced card players who watch closely may notice the unusual rhythm of the two passes.

docs.title: The Optical Shuffle (CC2 Ch. 17)
docs.text:
A shuffle that achieves the appearance of disorder without preserving any mathematical order mechanically. The right hand drops packets unevenly, the timing is varied, and the result looks maximally random. Yet the sequence has been arranged so the deck's order is preserved.

This is more of a handling approach than a mechanical false shuffle: the deception is primarily visual and psychological, exploiting the audience's association of visual disorder with genuine mixing.

docs.title: The Charlier Shuffle (CC5 Ch. 55)
docs.text: A one-handed shuffle variant attributed to Charlier, in which the deck is cascaded from one hand to the other. When used as a false shuffle, only specific portions of the deck are genuinely interleaved; other portions remain intact. Giobbi teaches this in CC5 as part of a set-up routine.

docs.title: The Overhand Lift Shuffle Run-up (CC5 Ch. 55)
docs.text: An advanced technique combining the lift shuffle with a stack run-up — building a specific order of cards on top of the deck through a series of apparently random overhand shuffles. This is covered under Stacking.

docs.title: The Red-Black Overhand Shuffle (CC5 Ch. 55)
docs.text: A false shuffle that preserves a complete red-black alternating sequence. Used specifically for tricks requiring a completely alternating deck that appears to have been mixed.

docs.title: What the Sources Agree On
docs.text: All volumes agree that overhand false shuffles should be performed at a natural pace without rushing. The failure mode of overhand false shuffles is hesitation — any break in rhythm signals to an attentive observer that something has changed.

docs.title: Related Articles
docs.text:
• False Shuffles — Overview
  • Overhand Shuffle Controls
  • Riffle False Shuffles
  • Stacking

docs.title: What This Is
docs.text: The overhand shuffle is the most natural-looking shuffle in casual handling — most non-magicians use it instinctively. Because the audience sees it as a genuine shuffle, it is also a powerful cover for secretly controlling a card. Giobbi devotes three separate chapters to overhand shuffle technique, progressing from basic to advanced.

docs.title: The Overhand Shuffle Itself
docs.text:
The deck is held in the right hand by the ends (end grip). The left thumb peels groups of cards off the top into the left hand, working from top to bottom. The cards arrive in the left hand in a reversed-then-reassembled order, which produces genuine mixing.

Giobbi notes several important points about the basic shuffle: - The left thumb should peel cards with a steady, relaxed rhythm — not in nervous bursts - Cards should fall with a slight overlap, not perfectly square — perfect squaring looks mechanical - The end position (cards returned to a squared deck in the left hand) must look identical before and after any control

docs.title: Running Single Cards
docs.text: Before any controls, Giobbi teaches "running" — the action of taking cards one at a time off the top rather than in packets. Running is used as part of every control. Mastering the feel of running single cards vs. packets is the technical prerequisite.

docs.title: Control of the Top and Bottom Cards (CC1 Ch. 2)
docs.text:
Top card control: At the start of the shuffle, the left thumb takes the very first card singly (runs one). This single card remains on the bottom of the left hand's growing packet. When the shuffle concludes and the right hand's remaining cards go on top, the formerly-top card is now the bottom of the reassembled deck. By doing this in reverse — running one at the end — the top card stays on top.

Bottom card control: By running all cards except the last, the bottom card of the original deck lands on top at the conclusion.

These are the simplest applications of overhand technique and are learned before any card-selection scenarios.

docs.title: The Injog Shuffle — Controlling the Top Stock
docs.text:
The injog is the core mechanism for controlling a spectator's card.

  1. The spectator replaces their card in the center of the deck
  2. The performer begins the overhand shuffle, taking the packet above the selected card normally
  3. The selected card is taken as a single injogged card — it protrudes slightly toward the performer
  4. Cards are shuffled off on top in ordinary packets
  5. The performer cuts at the injog, bringing everything above the injog (the former top stock) back on top of the selected card
  6. A final shuffle can run the selected card to the top

When executed with rhythm and without hesitation, this looks like a genuine shuffle to anyone who has not been taught to look for it. The key is that the injog must not be visible from the front, and the performer's eyes should not drop to the inner end of the deck.

Controlling the bottom stock: By running all cards above the inserted card individually (not in packets), then shuffling off the remainder, the original bottom stock can be kept intact on the bottom.

docs.title: The Diagonal Insertion (CC1 Ch. 4)
docs.text:
This is a subtler approach to control. When a spectator inserts their card into a spread, the performer's hands angle the deck so that the spectator's card goes in diagonally — one corner protruding at the inner end, the other at the outer.

When the spread is closed, only the protruding corner of the diagonal card is visible. The right hand takes the deck from above and uses the corner jog to maneuver the selected card to the top as the hands square the deck. No shuffle is needed; the control happens in the squaring action.

The diagonal insertion is elegant precisely because it creates no break and requires no subsequent shuffle — the control is instant.

docs.title: The Peek Control (CC1 Ch. 4)
docs.text:
When a spectator "peeks" at a card by riffling to a stop, the performer must control that card without knowing where it was peeked (since the spectator replaced it themselves). The peek control uses the mechanics of the riffle stop to establish a position, then shuttles the card to the top via a sequence of overhand moves.

Variants include a control for two simultaneously peeked cards and a multiple-peek control for three or more.

docs.title: The Lift Shuffle
docs.text:
A variation where the right hand lifts a packet from the bottom of the left hand's cards rather than peeling from the top. The visual effect is nearly identical to an overhand shuffle, but it allows different stock-preservation moves.

Top stock control with the lift shuffle and key-card placement with the lift shuffle are specific applications taught here.

docs.title: The G.W. Hunter Shuffle
docs.text: Named for G.W. Hunter. This shuffle controls the entire deck to its original order — a complete false shuffle that looks like genuine overhand mixing. It achieves this by exploiting the reversal properties of the overhand shuffle: two specific operations that individually would disorder the deck cancel each other out when combined.

docs.title: The Optical Shuffle
docs.text: A shuffle that looks deceptively random due to visual irregularity in the peel, but maintains the complete order of the deck. Unlike the Hunter shuffle (which uses mechanics), the optical shuffle uses the appearance of disorder without the mechanical properties that would create real mixing.

docs.title: What the Sources Agree On
docs.text: Giobbi consistently emphasizes that the overhand shuffle controls derive their deception not from technical perfection but from rhythm and context. A performer who never seems to look at the inner end of the deck, and who shuffles at the same pace before and after the control, will fool spectators even if the technique is not immaculate. Breaking rhythm is the most common mistake.

docs.title: Debate: Overhand vs. Riffle Shuffle Controls
docs.text:
The overhand shuffle is culturally more common in Europe; the riffle shuffle dominates in North America and among card players. Giobbi acknowledges this explicitly in CC1, noting that a spectator who grew up watching riffle shuffles may find an overhand shuffle more suspicious — not because the technique is weaker, but because it looks less familiar in their context.

No single shuffle is universally best. The performer should use the shuffle that looks most natural in their performing environment.

docs.text:
