docs.title: Edward Marlo
docs.text: Full name: Edward Marlo (1913–1991) Nationality: American Based in: Chicago, Illinois

docs.title: Who He Was
docs.text:
Edward Marlo was the most prolific creator of card sleights in the history of magic. A Chicago native who worked as a professional card player and later devoted himself entirely to magic creation, Marlo published an estimated ten thousand or more techniques over his lifetime through a continuous stream of booklets, manuscripts, and magazine contributions.

His approach was different from Vernon's: where Vernon favored perfecting a small number of techniques to absolute naturalness, Marlo's passion was invention — finding new mechanical solutions to problems, exploring variations, and pushing the technical boundaries of what was possible with cards.

docs.title: Techniques Attributed to Marlo in Giobbi's Series
docs.text:
Tilt (CC4 Ch. 50): Marlo's most widely-used technique — a method for apparently inserting a card into the middle of the deck while actually placing it second from the top. See Tilt.

The Olram Subtlety (CC3 Ch. 32): "Olram" is "Marlo" spelled backwards — a convention used specifically for Marlo attributions in magic literature. The Olram Subtlety is a handling refinement applied to the Elmsley Count that makes it more convincing. See False Counts and Displays.

Olram's Jinx Switch (CC4 Ch. 41): A packet switch technique. See Packet Switches.

docs.title: The "Olram" Convention
docs.text:
The use of "Olram" (Marlo reversed) as a pseudonymous attribution is a deliberate signal in magic literature: when you see "Olram," it means Marlo. The reason for the reversal is partly convention (Marlo reportedly used the pseudonym in some publications) and partly a marker in the literature that helps readers recognize the source.

Giobbi uses this convention correctly throughout the series.

docs.title: Marlo's Influence on Modern Card Technique
docs.text:
Marlo's influence is pervasive in modern card magic, even when his name is not explicitly cited. Many of the techniques now treated as "standard" — including various second deals, passes, double lifts, and packet sleights — were either invented or significantly refined by Marlo.

His publication method (constant small booklets and manuscripts) meant that his ideas circulated widely among card workers during his lifetime, and many techniques were absorbed into the general card magic vocabulary before their Marlo attribution was widely recognized.

docs.title: Marlo vs. Vernon: A Philosophical Debate
docs.text:
The broader magic community has often positioned Marlo and Vernon as representing two philosophical poles:

  • Vernon: Fewer techniques, deeply mastered, performance-focused, naturalness above all
  • Marlo: Vast technical invention, mechanically creative, interested in the problem of technique for its own sake

Giobbi clearly respects both traditions. His series teaches many Marlo techniques (Tilt, the Olram Subtlety) while structuring the overall course around Vernonian principles of quality and natural performance.

docs.title: Juan Tamariz
docs.text: Full name: Juan Tamariz Martel (born 1942) Nationality: Spanish Based in: Madrid

docs.title: Who He Is
docs.text:
Juan Tamariz is one of the greatest close-up magicians of the modern era and arguably the most creative card magician alive. He is a member of the Escuela Mágica de Madrid along with Arturo de Ascanio, and his approach to magic — playful, theatrical, deeply psychological — has been enormously influential worldwide.

Giobbi's connection to Tamariz is celebrated explicitly: in the dedication of CC1, Giobbi calls Tamariz "my role model as an artist and a human being." Tamariz wrote the foreword to Card College Volume 1 — an enthusiastic endorsement that positioned the book immediately as a serious work. Tamariz writes in the foreword that Giobbi "had to be someone with the deepest knowledge of the literature of card magic — in English, German, French, Italian and Spanish."

docs.title: Contributions Attributed to Tamariz in Giobbi's Series
docs.text:
The Secret Addition / Gaze Technique (CC1 Ch. 14): Giobbi explicitly credits Tamariz with the dynamic use of the gaze in the secret addition of cards — a specific technique for managing eye contact and visual attention during the loading of extra cards beneath a displayed packet. This contribution demonstrates Tamariz's philosophical approach: the technique is not a hand action but a perceptual action.

Tamariz is also the author of Sonata (Editorial Frakson, Madrid, 1991), cited in Giobbi's recommended reading list in CC2 — "In this book you will find many of the routines of one of the greatest close-up magicians of modern times."

docs.title: Tamariz's Theoretical Framework: The "Magical Trance"
docs.text:
Tamariz has written extensively about the psychology of magic. His most important contribution to theory — the idea of leading the spectator into what he calls a "magical trance" — describes the state where spectators stop analytically searching for explanations and surrender completely to the magical experience.

This aligns closely with Giobbi's own description of the ultimate goal of presentation: the feeling of "boundless astonishment" where spectators "simply believe you and give themselves over to the experience."

docs.title: Tamariz's Performance Style
docs.text:
Tamariz is famous for a highly interactive, theatrical, sometimes clownish performing style that is the antithesis of the cool, precise style associated with Vernon. Where Vernon advocated restraint and naturalness, Tamariz embraces expressiveness, humor, and theatrical exaggeration.

Both approaches serve the same ultimate goal — creating genuine wonder — through different paths. Giobbi presents both without privileging one, understanding that each approach is suited to different personalities and contexts.

docs.title: The Foreword to Card College Volume 1
docs.text: Tamariz's foreword is generous and specific. He identifies what makes Card College unique: it combines technical depth with theoretical rigor and practical performance wisdom in a way no previous book had achieved in English. He compares it favorably to the books of Jean Hugard and Frederick Braue, which had served as the standard English-language card magic course for a previous generation.

docs.title: Other Creators
docs.text: This article collects brief profiles of all other named originators that Giobbi credits throughout the Card College series.

docs.title: Jean-Eugène Robert-Houdin (1805–1871)
docs.text:
Nationality: French Contribution in Giobbi: The improved handling of the Top Change (CC1 Ch. 16). Giobbi attributes the specific top-change handling to Robert-Houdin and quotes him in the chapter epigraph: "The more simple and natural the movements of the performer, the less likely is the spectator to detect the trick."

Robert-Houdin is the most important figure in the history of stage magic. A clockmaker by training, he transformed magic performance from vulgar street spectacle into elegant theater and is often called "the father of modern magic." His memoirs were the inspiration for Harry Houdini's stage name.

docs.title: Frederick Braue (1908–1963)
docs.text:
Nationality: American Contributions in Giobbi:

  • The Braue Reverse (CC2 Ch. 24) — the foundational one-card reverse technique
  • The Braue Addition (CC1 Ch. 14) — a technique for secretly adding cards to a displayed packet

Braue co-authored The Royal Road to Card Magic (1948) with Jean Hugard — the book that served as the standard English-language card magic course before Card College. Juan Tamariz mentions this book in the CC1 foreword as Giobbi's predecessor.

docs.title: Alex Elmsley (1929–2006)
docs.text: Nationality: British Contribution in Giobbi: The Elmsley Count (CC2 Ch. 20) — the most widely used false count in close-up card magic. A mathematician who applied rigorous analytical thinking to card magic.

docs.title: Brother John Hamman (1916–1993)
docs.text: Nationality: American (Catholic brother) Contributions in Giobbi: - The Hamman Count (CC2 Ch. 20) - The Hamman Two-for-four Count (CC2 Ch. 20)

docs.title: Herb Zarrow (1920–2009)
docs.text: Nationality: American Contribution in Giobbi: The Zarrow Shuffle (CC3 Ch. 33) — the tabled riffle false shuffle that retains the complete deck order. See The Zarrow Shuffle.

docs.title: Harry Houdini (1874–1926)
docs.text:
Real name: Erik Weisz Nationality: American (Hungarian-born) Contribution in Giobbi: The Houdini Color Change (CC3 Ch. 37) — a color change in which the hand wipes across the face of the deck, leaving a different card revealed. See Color Changes.

Houdini's name is familiar to all audiences, which can be used to theatrical advantage when performing the Houdini Color Change with appropriate context.

docs.title: Jack Merlin (fl. early 20th century)
docs.text: Nationality: American Contribution in Giobbi: Jack Merlin's Riffle Shuffle (CC1 Ch. 12) — a one-handed spring-and-riffle shuffle performed as a flourish. It serves a dual purpose as both a visual display and a practical (if unusual) false shuffle.

docs.title: Cardini (1895–1973)
docs.text:
Real name: Richard Valentine Pitchford Nationality: Welsh-born, American Contribution in Giobbi: Cardini's Overhand Multiple Shift (CC3 Ch. 34) — a multiple shift performed during an overhand shuffle. See The Multiple Shift.

Cardini was famous for his silent manipulation act, performed while wearing monocle and white gloves.

docs.title: John Carney (born 1959)
docs.text: Nationality: American Contribution in Giobbi: Carney's Natural Multiple Shift (CC3 Ch. 34) — a version of the multiple shift emphasizing extreme naturalness of handling.

docs.title: Paul LePaul (1900–1958)
docs.text: Nationality: American Contribution in Giobbi: The LePaul Automatic Jog-control (CC3 Ch. 29) — a method for controlling an injogged card to the top during an overhand shuffle sequence. Named for Paul LePaul, author of The Card Magic of LePaul.

docs.title: Slydini (1901–1991)
docs.text:
Real name: Quintino Marucci (Tony Slydini) Nationality: Italian-born, American Contribution in Giobbi: The Strong/Weak Moments theory (CC2 Ch. 27) — the concept that every performance has moments of high and low audience attention, and that sleights should be executed during low-attention (weak) moments. Giobbi also cites Slydini's performance advice: "A good general chooses his battleground."

Slydini was known for lapwork and for seemingly impossible close-up magic achieved without apparent technique.

docs.title: Fred Kaps (1926–1980)
docs.text:
Nationality: Dutch Contribution in Giobbi: The Kaps Switch (CC4 Ch. 40) — a card switch technique. See Card Switches.

Fred Kaps was winner of the FISM Grand Prix (world magic championship) multiple times and is considered one of the finest close-up performers of the twentieth century.

docs.title: Ed Balducci
docs.text: Contribution in Giobbi: The Balducci Force / Crisscross Force (CC1 Ch. 5). See Other Forces.

docs.title: Goldin
docs.text: Contribution in Giobbi: The Goldin Force (CC1 Ch. 5) — a spread-based force method.

docs.title: Charles T. Jordan (1888–1944)
docs.text: Nationality: American Contribution in Giobbi: The Jordan Count (CC2 Ch. 20) — a false count that adds a card to a packet's apparent number.

docs.title: Norman Houghton
docs.text: Contribution in Giobbi: Norman Houghton's Pocket Extraction (CC3 Ch. 36) — a sequence for producing a palmed card from the jacket pocket. See Bottom Palm and Gambler's Cop.

docs.title: John Veeser
docs.text: Contribution in Giobbi: The Veeser Concept (CC4 Ch. 41) — the foundational principle underlying packet switches. See Packet Switches.

docs.title: Jean Hugard (1872–1959)
docs.text: Nationality: Australian, later American Note: Not credited for a specific technique in the Giobbi series, but Hugard's co-authored The Royal Road to Card Magic (with Frederick Braue) is referenced in the CC1 foreword by Tamariz as the previous generation's standard card magic course. Giobbi positions Card College as the successor to that tradition.

docs.title: Henry Christ
docs.text: Contribution in Giobbi: The Christ Twist (CC4 Ch. 49) — a specific pass variant using a distinctive wrist twist. See The Pass.

docs.title: Jean-Claude Pages
docs.text: Contribution in Giobbi: Jean-Claude Pages's Lapping Method (CC4 Ch. 48) — a natural-looking lapping technique by the French close-up worker. See Lapping.

docs.title: Paul Curry (1917–1986)
docs.text:
Nationality: American Contribution in Giobbi: The Curry Turnover Change (CC4 Ch. 40) — a card switch where a face-down card is turned over using another card as the lever, and the two exchange secretly. See Card Switches.

Curry is best known as the creator of the "Out of This World" effect, considered by many magicians to be one of the greatest card tricks ever devised.

docs.title: Nate Leipzig (1873–1939)
docs.text: Nationality: American (Swedish-born) Contribution in Giobbi: Not a technique attribution, but a performance philosophy: Giobbi cites Leipzig's advice in CC2 Ch. 27: "Don't ever perform unless coaxed." Leipzig was one of the first performers to develop a reputation for intimate, conversational close-up magic.

docs.title: S.W. Erdnase
docs.text: Pen name: S.W. Erdnase Real identity: Unknown — subject of ongoing historical debate Publication: The Expert at the Card Table (1902)

docs.title: Who He Was
docs.text:
S.W. Erdnase is the pseudonymous author of The Expert at the Card Table, published in Chicago in 1902. This book is, without question, the single most important text in the history of card technique — it documented gamblers' methods systematically for the first time and established the technical vocabulary still used today.

The identity of "S.W. Erdnase" has never been definitively established. Several historians have proposed candidates — the most often cited being E.S. Andrews (Erdnase reversed is roughly Andrews) — but no consensus exists. This remains one of the great mysteries of magic history.

Giobbi references Erdnase's work throughout the series, particularly in Volumes 3 and 4 where the most advanced techniques appear.

docs.title: Techniques Attributed to Erdnase
docs.text:
The Erdnase Break (CC3 Ch. 29): A specific break position described in The Expert at the Card Table. The Erdnase break involves a particular finger position that makes the break less visible than the standard little-finger break.

The Erdnase Stack Shuffle (CC4 Ch. 45): The foundational shuffle-stacking technique from The Expert at the Card Table. See Stacking.

False Dealing: The second deal and bottom deal techniques as taught in CC4 Ch. 46 are built on Erdnase's foundational documentation. See False Dealing.

docs.title: The Expert at the Card Table in Historical Context
docs.text:
Giobbi's foreword writer for CC4, writing from Strasbourg, makes a striking observation: Erdnase appeared in 1902, very nearly at the beginning of the twentieth century; Giobbi's fourth volume appears at its end. The parallel between the two major documents of card technique — spanning nearly a century — is not a coincidence of timing.

The Expert at the Card Table documented gambling technique primarily, not magic. It described how card cheats second-deal, bottom-deal, and stack decks. Its significance to magic was that it provided a rigorous technical vocabulary and methodology that magicians could study and adapt.

docs.title: Erdnase's Influence on Card Magic Vocabulary
docs.text: Many terms used throughout Card College trace directly to Erdnase's terminology: the "break," specific grip descriptions, the approach to the mechanics of shuffles. Even techniques not specifically from Erdnase are often described in Erdnase's vocabulary because it became the standard.

docs.title: The Mystery of the Identity
docs.text:
Giobbi does not attempt to resolve the Erdnase identity question — it is genuinely unresolved. The most seriously researched candidate at the time of Card College's publication was E.S. Andrews, but Giobbi notes this without endorsing it. Subsequent historical research has added additional candidates.

What matters for Card College's purposes: whether the author was a gambler, a magician, or both, the technical content of the book is accurate and has been verified by generations of card workers who can execute every technique it describes.
